content:
"Mard ko dard nahi hota." A man feels no pain. It's one of the first lessons many Pakistani boys learn, often meant as encouragement, a way of building resilience. But it also teaches boys early that emotional struggle isn't something to voice, and that lesson tends to follow them into adulthood, shaping how they cope, and whether they ever ask for help at all.

The numbers, where they exist, hint at what that silence costs. The World Health Organization recorded Pakistan's male suicide rate at 13.3 per 100,000 in 2019, compared to 4.3 per 100,000 among women. It's a gap worth sitting with rather than rushing past.

Part of the story is structural. A 2022 study in the Journal of the Pakistan Medical Association found Pakistan has just 0.19 psychiatrists per 100,000 people, among the lowest rates in the world. An estimated 24 million Pakistanis are thought to need psychiatric care, yet fewer than 500 psychiatrists serve the entire country, most concentrated in a handful of major cities. Roughly a fifth of trained psychiatrists also leave the country within five years of finishing training.

Funding tells a similar story. Around 0.4 percent of Pakistan's healthcare budget goes to mental health, much of it supporting a small number of psychiatric hospitals rather than everyday, accessible care. In a culture where masculinity is closely tied to being the family's economic anchor, struggling to keep up can feel like a personal failing rather than a health issue. Depression can look like exhaustion. Anxiety can look like irritability. None of it gets named as what it is.

The National Psychiatric Morbidity Survey of Pakistan, published in 2022, found that nearly 38 percent of the population has experienced a psychiatric disorder in their lifetime. Men are part of that number, yet research on help-seeking behavior suggests they're among the least likely to pursue formal treatment. It's not that men in Pakistan don't struggle. It's that the pathways to acknowledge that struggle are still being built, against a backdrop that has told them for generations not to look for one.

A national suicide registry, decoupled from the criminal penalties attempted suicide currently carries, would let researchers see the problem clearly for the first time. Expanding psychiatric care beyond major cities and modestly increasing mental health funding would help too.

None of this requires blaming any institution. It requires recognizing that Pakistani men are carrying a weight the system hasn't yet learned to see, and building the quiet infrastructure, and the permission, that would let them set some of it down.
comment: The policy gap is only one part; workplace culture matters too. Employers and community leaders need early signals and safe channels before distress turns into crisis.
